title: When One Says Tiruchi, Is It Not a Turning-Point Conference? (4)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The translation preserves the printed conference chronology, speeches, names and figures. Visually confirmed OCR repairs are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Merely compiling the conferences conducted by the DMK, those who presided and inaugurated them, and their stirring speeches would produce a vast history of party conferences. After the second State Conference at Tiruchi in 1956, the DMK held its third at Madurai on 13, 14, 15 and 16 July 1961 and its fourth at Chennai on 29, 30 and 31 December 1966 and 1 January 1967, both under Perarignar Anna.

Our dear friend Muthu chaired the reception committee for the third conference at Tirupparankundram in Madurai. K.A. Mathiazhagan inaugurated it, and as party treasurer I served as conference treasurer. On the first day, Ilatchiya Nadigar S.S. Rajendran performed *Thenpandi Veeran*; on the second, Nadippisai Pulavar K.R. Ramasamy performed *Tamil Vaazha Thalai Koduthaan*; and on the third, Makkal Thilagam M.G.R. performed *Sumai Thaangi*. I spoke on “The Yearning for Liberation.” Former Karnataka High Court Chief Justice Medappa, attending as a special observer, delivered an excellent English address about the DMK. I also reported on fundraising in my capacity as treasurer.

Anna's remarks on elections and alliances were significant: “Elections are important, but no party can abandon its basic principles for electoral advantage. At the 1956 Tiruchi State Conference, the people decided that the DMK should contest. It entered the general election, received more than seventeen lakh votes and seated fifteen members in the Assembly. Every impartial observer knows the valuable work the DMK has performed as the opposition. Our legislators' work has earned public esteem. The party has decided to continue its parliamentary activity and will contest the next general election by placing its principles before the people.”

He added: “I am not in a position to address you at length today. I stand here speaking, but my heart and thoughts are with those affected by the recent floods in Tiruchi and Thanjavur districts. I must go there soon. Before ending, I shall receive whatever you intend to give the flood victims and take it with me. It may be our first assistance. The people's joy and suffering have always been our purpose and concern. We cannot forget Thambi Karunanidhi's effort for the Thanjavur cyclone relief fund. He raised Rs. 25,000, which we provided as dhotis and saris. We must once again perform our duty. Those contributing to flood relief may come forward and give it to me.” This speech appears in *Perarignar Anna's Speeches*, published by Poompuhar Pathippagam.

Between the third State General Conference in 1961 and the fourth in 1966, the DMK conducted twenty conferences: eight district, five anti-Hindi, three student DMK, three regional DMK and one special election conference.

The fourth State General Conference was held at Virugambakkam before the 1967 Assembly election and occupies a distinguished place in DMK history. Anna presided, and I was given the opportunity to inaugurate it. Kanchi Kalyanasundaram hoisted the flag. Chinthanai Sirpi C.P. Chitrarasu opened the Martyrs' Fort, created in memory of those who sacrificed their lives for the party, while A. Govindasamy opened the art exhibition. The inaugural procession set out from Chennai's Island Grounds with approximately three lakh participants. Several lakhs more watched its splendour from buildings and tree branches along the route.

Navalar was conference organiser. Reception committee chairman was Neelanarayanan, whom I affectionately called “Neelam.” Secretaries were Mannai P. Narayanasamy, E.R. Krishnan, C.V.M. Annamalai and Ko. Senguttuvan, father-in-law of today's Parliamentary Party leader T.R. Baalu. K.M. Kannabiran, father-in-law of poet Vezhavendan, led the volunteer corps along with Su. Balan, S.V. Velu, Nallathambi, Sahul Hameed and Alamelu Appadurai.

The procession began at 3.30 p.m. and reached the pavilion named for Polur Subramaniam only at 9.30 p.m. *Dinamani*, then a neutral newspaper, wrote: “Only the ballot boxes can decide the DMK's influence in Chennai. That verdict will be known in the last week of February 1967. But if a verdict could be based on processions and demonstrations alone, one could say the DMK has already won the city. The procession was organised on such a gigantic scale.”

For a month before the conference, I toured election-fund meetings in every district, forgetting food and sleep, day and night. I felt neither the strain nor ordinary fatigue. Today, when our treasurer tours continuously, I think, “Alas, why must he wander so?” Whenever I returned to Chennai after those meetings, I would be beside Anna within half an hour.

On one such occasion, he simply told me, “Get into the car.” I did not ask where or why; I was not accustomed to questioning him so. Anna continued: “I have found a place for the conference and wanted to show you. That is where we are going.” The memory that Anna wished to take me personally to the site he had selected now overwhelms me and makes me want to cry aloud.

After several miles, the car stopped. Anna got out. It was a vast expanse without a person in sight. “This is the site selected for the conference,” he said. Even I was stunned. Everywhere were pits, mounds, heaps of stone like hilltops, and scattered trees, creepers and cactus thickets. “The conference must be held here. This must become beautiful ground fit for it,” Anna commanded. Once Anna had ordered it, what objection or hesitation could there be? Through comrades' hard labour, within days it became “Neelamegam Nagar.”

At the conference I said: “Friends, this place lay as wilderness, with black cobras crawling and scorpions and centipedes greeting anyone who touched it. In just fifteen days, we have transformed it into beautiful ground, a place where all of you can sit and rejoice, a conference pavilion.

“At the same time, dear friends, ask yourselves: if Arignar Anna commands, why cannot an administration transform this country, which has become a wilderness, into beautiful land? Let the echo of that question strike the eardrums of those who rule. Let them calculate that their time is over and prepare to receive their farewell notice from the people of Tamil Nadu.”

Though it was a DMK conference, statesman Rajaji, Quaid-e-Millath Ismail Sahib, Silambu Selvar M.P. Sivagnanam, and Kerala leaders Mohammed Koya and Thangal participated and added distinction. Rajaji said: “This alliance will certainly win. Never in my life have I seen people outside my party or grouping show me such affection. I see it before my eyes now. My heartfelt blessings to the DMK Thambis and their descendants.”

At Virugambakkam, as party treasurer, I had the great fortune of handing Anna eleven lakh rupees collected from the public for the election fund. The target was only ten lakhs, and there were moments when I myself wondered whether even that could be raised. Yet I exceeded it by one lakh.

When announcing candidates, Anna recalled this: “Thambi Karunanidhi gave me eleven lakh rupees. While speaking, he said his eyes had widened in wonder at whether it could be collected. Thambi, did your mother name you ‘Nidhi’ without reason? I have unshakable faith that Tamil words possess meaning. She named you ‘Nidhi’ so the people could use you as the nation's treasure. Had she lived to see this, how happy she would have been that your name had not gone to waste! Asked for ten lakhs, you gave eleven. Had the people been caught by you at the right moment, you would have collected still more!”

Nor did Anna stop there. Announcing the DMK candidates for the February 1967 election, he named each constituency and candidate. Then he said, “Saidapet...” paused for a moment, and declared, “Eleven lakhs.” Everyone in the pavilion rose in applause. It was not for the money I raised, but for the uniquely heartfelt way Perarignar Anna praised it. How many memories rise in waves and move me! Is there room to describe them all?

That fourth State General Conference became a great victory conference. It sowed the seeds of change, led to the DMK's massive electoral victory and brought it to power in Tamil Nadu. We did not then know that it would be Anna's last conference.

If I continue speaking only of the fourth, what of the tenth that must now be held? Its work proceeds steadily. Last Saturday, Thambi Nehru brought pavilion contractor Siva and artist J.P. Krishna to meet me. They showed me an album of pavilion photographs from many angles and a proposed list for the conference committees, prepared that morning with the treasurer, and received my approval.

Reception, finance, resolutions, souvenir, hospitality, publicity, pavilion, stage, decoration and advertising committees, volunteer, women's and women volunteers' wings, and conference-office officers have been selected and published in *Murasoli*. I trust every member will perform efficiently.

As I told reporters, arrangements are proceeding splendidly and systematically. You will have seen photographs of reception chairman K.N. Nehru setting out on a motorcycle to inspect the pavilion. Imagine how long it must be! He asked me to visit Tiruchi beforehand. Does he think his elder brother is sixty? Only if I doubted my Thambis' work would I need to inspect it in advance.

Trusting your arrival, we have announced that ten lakh people will assemble. Ensure that nothing obstructs your journey. Wedding invitations ask guests to come early with family, relatives and friends. I invite you to this conference likewise: come early with your family, relatives and friends, and help make both days a success!
Affectionately,
M.K.
19 January 2014
